summary:
The Great Ziggurat of Ur, constructed around 2100–2050 BC by Ur-Nammu and his son Shulgi of the [[event:1724|Third Dynasty of Ur]], is the best-preserved example of the monumental stepped temple platforms that defined Mesopotamian sacred architecture. Built from mud-brick with a fired-brick exterior, it rose in at least three ascending terraces and was dedicated to the moon god Nanna (Sin), the principal deity of the city of Ur. At its summit stood a temple shrine, though this upper level has not survived. The structure measured approximately 64 by 45 metres at its base and reached an estimated original height of 30 metres or more.

Ziggurats were the physical embodiment of Mesopotamian cosmology. They were conceived as cosmic mountains — artificial hills that replicated the primordial mound from which creation was believed to have emerged — and as stairways linking heaven and earth, allowing the gods to descend to the human world and priests to ascend toward the divine. The Great Ziggurat of Ur functioned as the administrative and religious centre of the city, with its associated temples, storehouses, and priestly residences forming a complex that controlled a significant portion of the city's economic life.

The ziggurat was restored and expanded in subsequent centuries. The Babylonian king Nabonidus undertook a major reconstruction in the sixth century BC, adding an additional tier and restoring the ceremonial stairways. This restoration ironically helped preserve the structure, which survived in substantially better condition than most other ancient Mesopotamian monuments. It was excavated and partly restored in the twentieth century by British archaeologist Leonard Woolley, who also excavated the nearby [[event:1727|Royal Cemetery of Ur]], and has since undergone further conservation and restoration work.

The Great Ziggurat of Ur remains one of the most evocative monuments of the ancient Near East. Its location in modern Iraq near the city of Nasiriyah has made it accessible to modern visitors, and its solid mud-brick core has proved remarkably durable. The ziggurat form it exemplifies influenced sacred architecture across Mesopotamia and is thought by some scholars to have inspired the biblical image of the Tower of Babel.
